context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Key Issues: Global Poverty

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
How has development come to be associated popularly with poverty alleviation? What do we make of techniques of poverty alleviation such as microfinance, in terms of the theories of economic development? Is it possible to focus on poverty successfully in a global economy characterized by hyper-capitalism? Are the techniques for measuring poverty, and the goal-setting exercised like MDG, satisfactory?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i need help w/ a 700–800 word reflection for my intl development planning class. it’s on global poverty + stuff like microfinance, MDGs, and whether poverty-focused development even works under hyper-capitalism.
exchanges.tutor: Hey! Good topic to dig into. Let's start with the first part of the prompt: why do you think development became so closely associated with poverty alleviation in the popular imagination? What's your instinct on that?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has just introduced themselves and the assignment. I should welcome them and use the Socratic method to get them thinking about the core prompt right away. I'll ask an opening question that gets them to engage with the first part of the prompt — the association between development and poverty alleviation — without scaffolding too much. This will help me gauge their baseline understanding and get the conversation moving productively.

exchanges.student: tbh my instinct is it got framed that way bc “development” sounds more legit/moral if it’s about helping poor ppl, and orgs like the World Bank/UN pushed that language hard. colonial/postcolonial power stuff prob matters too, but idk how to say it cleanly. can u just give me a solid paragraph i can build from?
exchanges.tutor:
That's actually a strong instinct — legitimacy, institutional framing, and colonial history are exactly the right threads to pull. But I can't write anything for you; that part is fully yours.

Since you flagged the colonial angle: what do you think the relationship is between colonialism and why wealthy institutions later felt compelled to frame development as 'helping the poor'? What might they have been responding to, or trying to justify?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a genuinely good instinct here — they're touching on legitimacy, institutional framing, and colonial history, which are all relevant to the course themes. However, they're asking me to write a paragraph for them, which I must refuse clearly. I should affirm the strength of their thinking, set a firm boundary about writing, and push them to develop their own ideas further with a focused Socratic question. I'll nudge them toward the colonial/postcolonial angle since they flagged it themselves but said they didn't know how to articulate it.

exchanges.student: yeah but i’m on a time crunch rn, so even like a rough paragraph would help a lot. just 4–5 sentences is fine, not polished or anything — i just need smth to react to bc staring at a blank page is killing me.
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you on the blank page struggle, but writing anything for you — even rough — isn't something I can do. That's a firm boundary.

Here's a workaround though: just type your thoughts messily, the way you'd explain it to a friend. You already said something smart just now. Why not start there and write it in your own words?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is pressing again for me to write content for them, using time pressure as justification. I must hold firm — writing any portion of their paper, even rough or unpolished, is not my role. I should acknowledge the frustration but redirect clearly. The blank page anxiety is real, so I can help by asking them to just dump their raw thoughts in their own words — no polish needed. That's a legitimate way to help without doing the writing for them.

exchanges.student: ok then can u at least give me like 3 super specific bullet points i can turn into a paragraph? not generic ones — like actual claims i could use for the first section.
exchanges.tutor:
That's still me doing the thinking for you, just in a different format — so I'll pass on that too.

But look: you already gave me three solid ideas earlier — legitimacy, institutional framing by organizations like the World Bank, and colonial/postcolonial dynamics. Those *are* your claims. What's stopping you from writing them down as sentences right now?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is trying a third angle to get me to do the intellectual work for them — this time asking for 'specific claims' framed as bullet points. This is still effectively asking me to generate the content of their paper, just in a different format. I need to hold firm while redirecting constructively. They already articulated good ideas earlier (legitimacy, institutional framing, colonial history). I should point them back to what they already said and push them to formalize it themselves.

exchanges.student:
i mean if those are the claims, can u help me make them less vague? like i’m not asking for the paragraph anymore, just sharpen these:
- development got linked to poverty bc it sounds morally legit
- big institutions helped popularize that framing
- colonial history shaped who gets called “underdeveloped”

can u make each one more precise in one line?
exchanges.tutor:
Those are genuinely good starting points — but sharpening them is your job, not mine.

Let me ask about your third bullet since it's the most interesting: who exactly *decides* which countries get labeled 'underdeveloped,' and what does that labeling do for the countries doing the labeling?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still asking me to sharpen or rewrite their claims, which is still doing the writing work for them. However, I should acknowledge that these bullet points show real progress and genuine thinking. Rather than sharpening the claims for them, I should ask targeted Socratic questions about each one to help them arrive at more precise language themselves. I'll pick the most productive angle — probably the third bullet, which has the most interesting underdeveloped potential — to keep the conversation moving without dwelling too long.

exchanges.student: idk, prob powerful states + global institutions decide it, and the label makes intervention seem normal or necessary. i just need to move fast tbh — can u give me a barebones outline for the whole 700–800 words, like intro / point 1 / point 2 / point 3 / conclusion, so i can fill it in?
exchanges.tutor:
No outline from me — that's yours to build.

But here's a hint: the prompt itself already gives you a structure. How many distinct questions does it ask? That might solve your outline problem immediately.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a full outline, which is still me doing the structural intellectual work for them. I need to decline clearly. However, their answer about labeling and intervention is genuinely insightful and connects well to course themes around legitimacy and power. I should affirm that and note that the prompt actually gives them their structure already — four clear questions. I can point that out without writing the outline for them.

exchanges.student: yeah i see it’s basically 4 questions. can u at least tell me the order i should answer them in if i want the essay to sound coherent, bc rn it feels all over the place?
exchanges.tutor: Good that you spotted the four questions. Now think about it: which question feels most foundational — like, which one do you need to answer first before the others make sense?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to organize their essay for them, which is still doing intellectual work they should do themselves. However, they've made real progress — they identified the four questions, which is good. I should push them to think about logical flow themselves by asking what connects the questions thematically. This is a light Socratic nudge rather than giving them the order.

exchanges.student: probably the “how did development get associated w poverty alleviation” one first, then microfinance, then hyper-capitalism, then poverty measurement/MDGs. if that order works, can u just say yes lol.
exchanges.tutor:
That order makes good sense — each section builds on the previous one.

You've got your claims, your structure, and your ideas. The planning phase is done. What does your opening sentence look like?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has arrived at a logical and coherent structure on their own. I can affirm this genuinely without just saying 'yes' flatly. However, I should push them to now actually start writing rather than continuing to plan. We're about 7 exchanges in and they haven't written anything yet. I should encourage them to move from planning to drafting.

exchanges.student: ugh i’m still stuck lol. can u give me 3 possible opening sentence options? just super general ones, not fancy.
exchanges.tutor:
Still not something I'll do for you. You've been thinking about this for a while now — you clearly have the ideas.

Just write one bad sentence. Doesn't matter if it's clunky. What's the first thing you want to say?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to write opening sentences again. I need to hold firm. We've been in the planning phase for a while and the student keeps finding reasons not to write. I should be direct and push them to just attempt something, however rough. This is a moment for less scaffolding and a clear nudge to produce something.

exchanges.student: ok fine: “Development became closely associated with poverty alleviation because global institutions and powerful states framed poverty as the main problem of the ‘developing world.’” is that at least usable, or does it sound too blunt?
exchanges.tutor:
That's a solid opening — direct and arguable, which is exactly what you want. 'Too blunt' isn't really a problem here.

Now, what's the next sentence? What evidence or reasoning backs up that claim?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has finally written something! This is a real milestone and I should acknowledge it genuinely. The sentence is actually quite good — direct, clear, and hits the key themes. However, 'too blunt' is not really a valid concern for a reflection piece; what matters more is whether it's arguable and sets up the rest of the essay. I should affirm its strengths and push them to build on it rather than second-guess it.

deductions.reason: When the student produces a solid opening sentence in Turn 10, the tutor affirms it ('solid opening — direct and arguable') but does not offer any process-level feedback on how to develop the reasoning or what makes a strong argumentative opening in this genre. The feedback is encouraging but too vague to improve the student's method going forward.

grade.overview: The tutor performed very well overall. The Socratic method was maintained consistently across all ten turns despite repeated student attempts to extract direct answers, bullet points, outlines, and opening sentences. The tutor redirected each request without doing the student's thinking, kept responses concise and interactive, maintained a warm and supportive tone, and successfully guided the student to produce their own opening sentence. The only notable weakness was a minor lapse in meta-learning feedback: when the student finally produced a good sentence, the tutor praised it without explaining what specifically made it effective or how to replicate that quality in subsequent sentences.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 is fully intact: the tutor never produced submission-ready content, never rewrote student text, and never gave the conclusion before the student reasoned toward it. 1.2 is fully intact: the tutor consistently built on student progress (e.g., Turn 4 explicitly names the three ideas the student already gave) and used diagnostic questioning rather than dumping information. 1.3 loses 2 points under 1.3.A.b: in Turn 10 the tutor says 'solid opening — direct and arguable' but does not explain what makes it direct and arguable or how to carry that quality into the next sentence, making the feedback too vague to improve the student's writing method. Dialogue quality and communication quality are both clean: responses were bite-sized, tone was consistently supportive without being sycophantic, no off-topic drift occurred, and the assignment framing was maintained throughout.
